note_en: You will drink it faster than you expect, and you may end up sharing it.

label_en: ORS sachets, or salt and sugar
note_en: Water alone does not replace what you lose sweating in this heat.
Dry snacks, nuts, biscuits. Something for a friend too.

label_en: Cap, umbrella, or light long sleeves
note_en: Shade you carry with you. Long sleeves in light cotton beat bare arms in direct sun.
Bandages, antiseptic, gauze, and a clean cloth. Enough to stop bleeding and cover a wound.
Carry more than one day's worth in case you cannot get home. If you use an inhaler, bring it and keep it on you, not in a bag you might lose.
Tear gas gets trapped under a lens against your eye. Wear glasses if you can.

label_en: A scarf or cloth, and clean water for eyes
note_en: A separate small bottle kept clean, so you can flush your eyes without using your drinking water.

label_en: Charged phone and a power bank
note_en: Turn the brightness down and close what you are not using. Heat drains batteries fast.
For writing an emergency number on your arm, and on a friend's.
Enough to get home. Digital payments fail when the network is shut down.
Being able to establish who you are is usually helpful, including to whoever is trying to find you.
Tear gas gets trapped underneath and keeps burning. Wear glasses instead.
Tear gas powder sticks to oil and stays on your skin. Go with a clean, dry face.

label_en: Anything sharp, or anything that could be called a weapon
note_en: The right to assemble is protected when it is peaceful and without arms. Do not give anyone a reason to say otherwise.

label_en: Valuables you cannot afford to lose
note_en: Things get dropped, crushed, and taken. Bring only what you can walk away without.
It slows your judgement when you need it most, and hands over an easy separate charge.
